issue: Researcher/dossiers/ai_agents.md passes at 0.80 — high source quality, intact cross-source agreement, current freshness, complete handoffs. Stage discipline drags the score: three claims (clm-2026-06-02-004, 005, 006) carry weight >= 0.7 with verified=false in claims.jsonl, yet the dossier presents them as established fact. Underlying primary sources exist; verification just has not been recorded.
narrative: The dossier is real, dense, and useful. The gap is internal: claims with primary-source backing have not been promoted from verified=false to verified=true in the claims ledger, and the dossier prose does not flag the difference. Recommend a focused verification sweep: clm-2026-06-02-004 (LangChain 1.0 standard — src-003, src-006, src-015 all primary), clm-2026-06-02-005 (persistent memory frontier — src-017, src-018 both primary arXiv), clm-2026-06-02-006 (memory security class — src-018 primary arXiv). All three could pass verification against existing sources. Until then, dossier prose should mark these as 'high-weight, unverified' rather than asserting as fact.

issue: Claim clm-2026-06-02-007 (Artemis II flew Apr 1, 2026) is asserted at weight 0.99 from a single secondary source (Researcher synthesis) with zero primary sources captured in the trail; the synthesis report itself acknowledges primary sources are cited-but-not-captured (NASA press release for Artemis II success is the obvious target). Audit policy hard rule violated: weight >= 0.6 requires >= 1 primary source in trail. Underlying event is high-confidence (publicly announced and historical), but dossier-grade promotion needs the primary capture.
narrative: What was checked: source record src-2026-06-03-001 (type=secondary, trust=0.7, self-referential URL, honest trust_notes); finding fnd-2026-06-03-001 (single source_id, claim collapses 4 separate factual claims); claim clm-2026-06-02-007 (weight 0.99, verified=false, supporting_findings=[fnd-2026-06-03-001]); report reports/artemis_status_mid2026.md (11 lines, no inline primary citations); dossier dossiers/artemis_program.md (template consistent with sibling dossiers but contains 2 factual errors: line 20 references fnd-artemis-2026-06-03-001 which is not the actual finding ID, and claims the finding is not in findings.jsonl when it is). Cross-source agreement is partial: the synthesis references multiple primaries internally but they are not independently captured. Stage discipline: raw capture missing (no raw/aerospace/ folder); no run receipt for the 2026-06-03 artemis pass; 'aerospace' lane is not in the Researcher's documented source plan. Freshness: data is fresh (fetched 2026-06-03T20:30) but the cadenced window for the lane is undefined. Handoff hygiene: dossier correctly routes to Verifier audit lane, no premature promotion. Verdict: NEEDS-MORE-EVIDENCE — the gap is named (missing primary source), the audit policy hard rule is cited, and the recommended action is concrete (capture NASA Artemis II success press release as a new primary source).

issue: Claim clm-2026-06-02-008 (Artemis III restructured May 13, 2026 from lunar landing to Earth-orbit docking test, late 2027; landing pushed to Artemis IV 2028) is asserted at weight 0.98 from the same single secondary synthesis with zero primary captures. Same policy violation as clm-2026-06-02-007: weight >= 0.6 requires >= 1 primary source. Claim contains both a verifiable historical event (May 13 restructure announcement) and a forward-looking target (late 2027 docking test); the restructure is the verifiable part and the obvious primary target is NASA's own May 13 announcement.
narrative: What was checked: same trail as 007 (identical source, finding, and report). The claim is structurally more complex (announcement + restructure + new target + knock-on to Artemis IV), which is a second-order concern: the underlying finding collapses four related but distinct facts (Artemis II success + Artemis III restructure + Artemis IV push + HLS progress) into a single finding, then the claim layer carves out one slice. The dossier itself flags the most important re-verification question: 'Are the late-2027 orbital test and 2028 landing dates firm, or aspirational? Apollo-era schedule history suggests the latter.' That is the right question; the answer requires the NASA primary source. Verdict: NEEDS-MORE-EVIDENCE — same gap, same action, same score. If 008 is later split into 'restructure announced' (verifiable) and 'late 2027 target' (forward-looking) components, the verifiable half should re-audit cleanly once the primary source is captured.

issue: Claim clm-2026-06-02-009 (HLS providers racing 2027 deadline; SpaceX Starship test late May 2026; propellant transfer demos later 2026; Blue Origin Blue Moon on parallel track) is asserted at weight 0.85 — already 0.13 below 007/008 — but aggregates three different factual claims (SpaceX test, SpaceX propellant transfer demo, Blue Origin Blue Moon status) into one claim, and the underlying single source is a synthesis with no primary captures. The dossier's own 'Contradictions and open questions' section flags Blue Moon: 'Blue Moon status is described at the same level as Starship HLS, but the public cadence is much quieter. Is Blue Moon on a parallel critical path or a backup?' That open question is not resolved by the trail.
narrative: What was checked: same source trail as 007/008. The claim is the loosest of the three (per the parent's prior observation): the SpaceX Starship test claim is the most concrete (specific event, late May 2026, presumably SpaceX press release / NASASpaceflight coverage available); the propellant transfer demo is forward-looking ('later in 2026' is vague); the Blue Origin Blue Moon 'parallel track' assertion is the weakest — the dossier itself is not sure Blue Moon is on a parallel critical path vs. a backup. Cross-source agreement scored lower (0.40 vs 0.50 for 007/008) because the synthesis internal mentions SpaceX more than Blue Origin. Recommend splitting the claim into 3 sub-claims (SpaceX test, SpaceX propellant demo, Blue Origin status) and capturing primary sources for each before re-adjudication. Verdict: NEEDS-MORE-EVIDENCE — same primary-source gap, plus the split_claim action to disentangle the components. Lowest score of the three (0.455) reflects the looser foundation, matching the parent session's prior observation.
